frames.description: the ground truth — what actually happened in the night
what the town of Harlow Mill holds to be true

branches.description: Sela opens the sluice; the pond bleeds, the dam holds, the lower row drowns
Sela holds the sluice and rides to wake the lower row; the dam overtops, the mill is wrecked
the shared dawn both paths reach: the mill-yard reckoning and the river's edge

entities.description: Sela Harrow, the gatekeeper's daughter, keeper in fact since her father's stroke
Garrick Vane, the mill's owner, whose dawn word is the town's verdict
the old keeper, voiceless in a back room since his stroke
the poorest cottages, downstream of the sluice, that flood first
the upstream relief gate, the safe way to bleed the pond
the mill's flood-sluice, which only the keeper may open
the old dam upstream, cresting in the once-a-generation storm
Harlow Mill, the town's failing living

predicates.description: the standing of the relief gate's truth (wedged / wedged-on-purpose / surfaced)
who the keeper's authority over the sluice is held to belong to
Sela's standing before the town: accused or keeper
the physical state of the mill's sluice in the night (shut / open)
the fate the night dealt (asleep / drowned / woken / wrecked)

facts.claim: the once-a-generation storm crests the old dam upstream and the mill's swelling pond rises toward the lip in the dark
Sela finds the upstream relief gate wedged shut, so the pond cannot drain the safe way
the relief gate was wedged earlier in the season, on purpose, to keep the pond high for the mill's failing wheel, and was never freed
Sela has been the keeper in fact since her father's stroke; by law and the owner's word, only the keeper may open the sluice
the town still calls Sela's father the keeper and Sela a girl playing at his work, with no authority to open the sluice
at the sluice a millhand who holds the father still keeper tries to stop Sela's hand, calling for the old keeper to be fetched
with the relief gate wedged, the dam will overtop within the hour unless the mill's own sluice is opened
Sela reaches the sluice with the crest minutes away; the lower row sleeps in the dark downstream
the town has not yet judged Sela; the night's act will be put to Garrick at dawn, and she stands accused until he speaks
at the sluice Sela faces one exclusive choice she cannot unmake: open the sluice, or hold it and ride to wake the lower row
Sela throws the sluice open; she is the hand that opens the water onto the poorest
the pond bleeds and the dam holds, but the lower row floods first in the dark and lives are lost there
the dam holds and the mill is spared; survivors climb from the lower-row rooftops as the pond falls back behind the open sluice
at first grey the lower-row dead are counted and the town's mood turns hard, blaming the hand that opened the water rather than the wedged gate it could not yet see
Sela holds the sluice shut and rides into the dark to wake the lower row herself
warned in the dark, the lower row flees its cottages onto the high ground above the valley
no one is deliberately drowned, but the dam overtops, the valley takes the flood unshaped, and the mill itself is wrecked
at first grey no body is pulled from the lower row, but the mill stands broken and the town's living with it; the mood is grief, not blame
at the reckoning the lower-row dead are named aloud and mourned, and Sela stands as the hand that opened the water onto them
at the reckoning the wrecked mill stands witness, and Sela is the keeper who spent the town's living to drown no one
at the reckoning the saved lower row stands whole in the yard, the cost of the wrecked mill paid so that none of them drowned
at first light the town gathers in the mill yard and Garrick must say aloud whether Sela acted as keeper or as criminal
the wedged-gate truth surfaces at the reckoning: the danger was made by whoever wedged the relief gate, not by Sela alone, and this turns Garrick's verdict
Garrick names Sela's act a keeper's duty, not a crime; his word is the town's verdict
the town's belief that Sela was only a girl playing at her father's work breaks when Garrick names her keeper before them all
after the verdict Sela walks alone to the river's edge in the grey light and looks at the water that did what it did
